title: Chapter 4 – Advanced Data Modeling Practice Quiz

questions.question: What does ERD stand for and what does it represent?
questions.answer: Entity-Relationship Diagram; it represents a conceptual database

questions.question: What is a collection of entities that share common properties or characteristics called?
What term refers to the actual population of data instances at a specific moment?
What is a single occurrence of an entity type called?
What type of entity is existent-independent and always has a unique identifier?
What type of entity is existent-dependent and cannot exist without a relationship with another entity?
What is the entity type that a weak entity depends on called?
What term refers to the set of possible values an attribute can hold?
What type of attribute uniquely identifies each entity instance?
What type of identifier is a primary key composed of more than one attribute?
What type of attribute can be subdivided into smaller components?
What type of attribute cannot be subdivided?
What type of attribute can hold more than one value for a single entity instance?
What type of attribute has its value computed from other attributes?
What normal form is violated when multivalued attributes or M:N relationships are implemented directly in a relational table?
What type of key is a simple or composite key that is unique and minimal?
What type of key consists of all candidate keys not chosen as the primary key?
What type of key references the primary key in another table and can be null?
What term describes the minimum and maximum number of entity occurrences associated with one occurrence of a related entity?
What term describes the relationship classification (1:1, 1:M, M:N)?
What type of participation requires that one entity occurrence must have a corresponding entity occurrence?
What type of participation allows an entity occurrence to exist without a corresponding entity occurrence?
What relationship degree involves only one entity associated with itself?
What relationship degree involves two entities being associated?
What relationship degree involves three entities being associated?
What type of relationship occurs when an entity has a relationship with itself?
What type of entity is used to implement M:N relationships and is also known as a bridge entity?
In Chen notation, how is an attribute represented?
questions.answer: An oval connected to a rectangle (entity)

questions.question: What must be true about the data types of a foreign key and the primary key it references?
questions.answer: They must be of the same data type

questions.question: What is the first step in developing an ER diagram?
questions.answer: Create a detailed narrative of the organization's description of operations

questions.question: What is a generic entity type that is related to one or more entity subtypes and contains common characteristics?
What is the property by which subtype entities inherit values of all attributes and all relationships of the supertype?
What is the top-down process of identifying lower-level, more specific entity subtypes from a higher-level entity supertype?
What is the bottom-up process of identifying a higher-level, more generic entity supertype from lower-level entity subtypes?
What completeness constraint, symbolized by a double line, means that every supertype occurrence must be a member of at least one subtype?
What completeness constraint, symbolized by a single line, means that not every supertype occurrence is a member of a subtype?
What are subtypes that contain a unique subset of the supertype entity set, where each supertype instance can appear in only one subtype, called?
What are subtypes that contain nonunique subsets of the supertype entity set, where a supertype instance may appear in more than one subtype, called?
What is the attribute in the supertype entity that determines to which subtype the supertype occurrence is related?
What is a real-world identifier used to uniquely identify real-world objects and is familiar to end users called?
What type of primary key is created to simplify the identification of entity instances, has no business meaning, and exists only to distinguish one entity from another?
What design trap occurs when one entity is in two 1:M relationships to other entities, producing an association among other entities not expressed in the model?
What type of data has values that change over time and requires keeping a history of data changes?
What occurs when there are multiple relationship paths between related entities?
What depiction shows the arrangement of higher-level entity supertypes (parent entities) and lower-level entity subtypes (child entities) and describes 'is-a' relationships?
Which of the following correctly describes an entity type in a relational environment?
choices: A) A single row in a table
B) A single entity occurrence
C) A table, not a single row
D) A foreign key reference
questions.answer: C) A table, not a single row

questions.question: Which of the following is true about a strong entity type?
choices: A) It depends on another entity for its existence
B) Its primary key is derived from a parent entity
C) It is existent-independent and always has a unique identifier
D) It cannot exist without a foreign key
questions.answer: C) It is existent-independent and always has a unique identifier

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a weak entity?
choices: A) EMPLOYEE
B) PRODUCT
C) ORDER_ITEM
D) CUSTOMER

questions.question: What does the identifying owner of a weak entity provide?
choices: A) The foreign key for the weak entity
B) The primary key from which the weak entity's key is derived
C) The optional participation rule
D) The domain of the weak entity's attributes
questions.answer: B) The primary key from which the weak entity's key is derived

questions.question: Which attribute type must always contain a value?
choices: A) Optional attribute
B) Derived attribute
C) Required attribute
D) Multivalued attribute

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a composite attribute?
choices: A) AGE
B) SEX
C) ADDRESS (Street, City, Zip Code)
D) SERIAL_NUMBER

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a simple attribute?
choices: A) ADDRESS
B) FULL_NAME (First, Last)
C) DEGREE
D) MARITAL_STATUS

questions.question: Why should multivalued attributes NOT be implemented directly in relational tables?
choices: A) They increase the number of foreign keys
B) Relational tables require atomic attributes and M:N relationships violate 1NF
C) They conflict with the candidate key
D) They prevent the use of derived attributes
questions.answer: B) Relational tables require atomic attributes and M:N relationships violate 1NF

questions.question: When handling a multivalued attribute in relational design, what happens to the original entity's primary key?
choices: A) It becomes a candidate key in the new table
B) It is deleted and replaced
C) It becomes a foreign key in the new table
D) It becomes an alternate key
questions.answer: C) It becomes a foreign key in the new table

questions.question: Which key type includes ALL candidate keys that were NOT selected as the primary key?
choices: A) Foreign key
B) Composite key
C) Alternate key
D) Surrogate key

questions.question: What is the purpose of a foreign key in a relational table?
choices: A) To uniquely identify each record in its own table
B) To reference the primary key of another table and preserve referential integrity
C) To serve as an alternate identifier when no primary key exists
D) To store derived attribute values
questions.answer: B) To reference the primary key of another table and preserve referential integrity

questions.question: What does cardinality express in a relationship?
choices: A) The type of entity involved in a relationship
B) The minimum and maximum number of entity occurrences associated with one occurrence of a related entity
C) Whether participation is optional or mandatory
D) The number of attributes in an entity
questions.answer: B) The minimum and maximum number of entity occurrences associated with one occurrence of a related entity

questions.question: Which Crow's Foot symbol represents a cardinality of (0,N)?
choices: A) One and only one
B) Zero or one
C) Zero or many
D) One or many

questions.question: Which Crow's Foot symbol represents a cardinality of (1,1)?
choices: A) Zero or many
B) One or many
C) One and only one
D) Zero or one

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a ternary relationship?
choices: A) EMPLOYEE manages EMPLOYEE
B) STUDENT enrolls in CLASS
C) VENDOR supplies PART to WAREHOUSE
D) EMPLOYEE is assigned to PARKING SPACE
questions.answer: C) VENDOR supplies PART to WAREHOUSE

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an associative entity?
choices: A) To represent a weak entity that depends on a strong entity
B) To implement M:N relationships between entities
C) To store derived attribute values
D) To replace the primary key of an entity
questions.answer: B) To implement M:N relationships between entities

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between entity type and entity set?
choices: A) Entity type is the actual data; entity set is the schema
B) Entity type is the definition/schema; entity set is the actual collection of data instances
C) Entity type and entity set are the same concept
D) Entity set refers to a single occurrence; entity type refers to multiple occurrences
questions.answer: B) Entity type is the definition/schema; entity set is the actual collection of data instances

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct order of steps when developing an ER diagram?
choices: A) Develop ERD → Identify entities → Write business rules → Revise ERD
B) Identify attributes → Write narrative → Develop ERD → Identify entities
C) Write narrative → Identify business rules → Identify entities → Develop ERD → Identify attributes → Revise ERD
D) Identify primary keys → Develop ERD → Write narrative → Identify relationships
questions.answer: C) Write narrative → Identify business rules → Identify entities → Develop ERD → Identify attributes → Revise ERD

questions.question: Why is a derived attribute generally not physically stored in the database?
choices: A) It violates the First Normal Form
B) It can be calculated from other attributes, avoiding redundancy and update anomalies
C) It conflicts with the primary key
D) It is always a multivalued attribute
questions.answer: B) It can be calculated from other attributes, avoiding redundancy and update anomalies

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct example of handling a STUDENT entity with a multivalued DEGREE attribute in relational design?
choices: A) STUDENT (StudentID, Name, Degree1, Degree2)
B) STUDENT (StudentID, Name) and STUDENT_DEGREE (StudentID, Degree)
C) DEGREE (DegreeID, StudentID) with no STUDENT table
D) STUDENT (StudentID, Name, Degree) with a composite key
questions.answer: B) STUDENT (StudentID, Name) and STUDENT_DEGREE (StudentID, Degree)

questions.question: Which of the following best describes an entity supertype?
choices: A) A specialized entity that holds unique characteristics
B) A generic entity type related to one or more subtypes that contains common characteristics
C) An entity that can only exist as part of another entity
D) An entity with no relationships to other entities
questions.answer: B) A generic entity type related to one or more subtypes that contains common characteristics

questions.question: What does attribute inheritance mean in the context of supertype/subtype relationships?
choices: A) Supertypes receive attribute values from subtypes
B) Subtype entities inherit values of all attributes and relationships of the supertype
C) Attributes are duplicated in both supertype and subtype tables
D) Only the primary key is shared between supertype and subtype
questions.answer: B) Subtype entities inherit values of all attributes and relationships of the supertype

questions.question: Which process involves identifying a VEHICLE supertype by grouping the common attributes of CAR, TRUCK, and MOTORCYCLE?
choices: A) Specialization
B) Normalization
C) Generalization
D) Decomposition

questions.question: Which process involves breaking down a PART entity into MANUFACTURED PART and PURCHASED PART?
choices: A) Generalization
B) Specialization
C) Normalization
D) Aggregation

questions.question: A VEHICLE entity type is connected to CAR and TRUCK subtypes with a single line. What does this indicate?
choices: A) Total completeness – every vehicle must be a car or a truck
B) Partial completeness – a vehicle does not have to be a car or a truck
C) Disjoint constraint – a vehicle can be both a car and a truck
D) Overlapping constraint – a vehicle must belong to multiple subtypes
questions.answer: B) Partial completeness – a vehicle does not have to be a car or a truck

questions.question: A PATIENT entity type is connected to OUTPATIENT and RESIDENT PATIENT subtypes with a double line. What does this mean?
choices: A) Partial completeness – a patient may or may not belong to a subtype
B) Total completeness – every patient must be either an outpatient or a resident patient
C) Overlapping constraint – a patient can be both an outpatient and a resident patient
D) Disjoint constraint – a patient can belong to more than one subtype
questions.answer: B) Total completeness – every patient must be either an outpatient or a resident patient

questions.question: A disjoint constraint is represented by which symbol inside the specialization circle?
choices: A) O
B) D
C) X
D) S

questions.question: In the EMPLOYEE supertype with subtypes HOURLY EMPLOYEE, SALARIED EMPLOYEE, and CONSULTANT, the Employee Type attribute with values 'H', 'S', or 'C' is an example of what?
choices: A) Composite identifier
B) Alternate key
C) Subtype discriminator
D) Surrogate key

questions.question: Which of the following describes the specialization hierarchy rule regarding attribute placement?
choices: A) All attributes should be placed at the lowest possible level of the hierarchy
B) Attributes are assigned at the highest logical level possible in the hierarchy
C) Attributes can only be assigned to the root supertype
D) Each subtype must have the same attributes as the supertype
questions.answer: B) Attributes are assigned at the highest logical level possible in the hierarchy

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a candidate key?
choices: A) A key that references another table's primary key
B) A key composed of more than one attribute
C) A simple or composite key that is unique and minimal
D) A key that stores computed values
questions.answer: C) A simple or composite key that is unique and minimal

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a desirable characteristic of a primary key?
choices: A) Unique values
B) Contains meaningful business information (intelligent)
C) No change over time
D) Preferably numeric

questions.question: Composite primary keys are most appropriate in which two scenarios?
choices: A) As identifiers for strong entities and as surrogate keys
B) As identifiers for composite (associative) entities and as identifiers for weak entities
C) As alternate keys and as foreign keys
D) As natural keys and as derived keys
questions.answer: B) As identifiers for composite (associative) entities and as identifiers for weak entities

questions.question: A surrogate key is best described as which of the following?
choices: A) A composite key created from multiple business attributes
B) A key with no business meaning created solely to identify entity instances
C) A natural key already used in day-to-day business operations
D) A key that references a parent entity's primary key
questions.answer: B) A key with no business meaning created solely to identify entity instances

questions.question: When is it most appropriate to use a surrogate primary key?
choices: A) When a natural key already exists and is short
B) When the composite key would be too lengthy or cumbersome to use as a foreign key
C) When the entity has no relationships with other entities
D) When the entity type has overlapping subtypes
questions.answer: B) When the composite key would be too lengthy or cumbersome to use as a foreign key

questions.question: In a 1:M relationship, where is the foreign key placed?
choices: A) On the 'one' side as the primary key
B) On the 'many' side, referencing the primary key of the 'one' side
C) In both entities
D) In a separate linking table
questions.answer: B) On the 'many' side, referencing the primary key of the 'one' side

questions.question: In a 1:1 relationship where one side is mandatory and the other is optional, where should the foreign key be placed?
choices: A) In the entity on the mandatory side
B) In the entity on the optional side
C) In both entities
D) In a separate associative entity
questions.answer: B) In the entity on the optional side

questions.question: Which design case involves creating a new entity in a 1:M relationship with the original entity to store previous values along with dates of change?
choices: A) Design Case 1 – Implementing 1:M Relationships
B) Design Case 2 – Maintaining History of Time-Variant Data
C) Design Case 3 – Fan Traps
D) Design Case 4 – Redundant Relationships
questions.answer: B) Design Case 2 – Maintaining History of Time-Variant Data

questions.question: In the fan trap example involving TEAM, DIVISION, and PLAYER, what was the root cause of the fan trap?
choices: A) Both TEAM and PLAYER had the same primary key
B) PLAYER's foreign key pointed to DIVISION instead of TEAM, misidentifying the direct relationship
C) DIVISION had too many attributes
D) TEAM was modeled as a weak entity
questions.answer: B) PLAYER's foreign key pointed to DIVISION instead of TEAM, misidentifying the direct relationship

questions.question: What is the main concern with redundant relationships in a database design?
choices: A) They always cause fan traps
B) They must remain consistent across the model to avoid anomalies
C) They violate the First Normal Form
D) They prevent the use of foreign keys
questions.answer: B) They must remain consistent across the model to avoid anomalies

questions.question: In the Tiny College scenario, why was a JOB_HIST entity created?
choices: A) To resolve a fan trap between PROFESSOR and SCHOOL
B) To track the history of administrative appointments over time
C) To implement an M:N relationship between PROFESSOR and DEPARTMENT
D) To eliminate a redundant relationship between SCHOOL and EMPLOYEE
questions.answer: B) To track the history of administrative appointments over time
